content.text: To maintain Eduversal Partner Schools' status as approved Cambridge Assessment International Education Centres, ensure examination integrity, deliver high-quality Cambridge curriculum and assessments, and comply with all Cambridge policies and procedures.
A Cambridge Centre is a school or institution approved by Cambridge Assessment International Education to:

items: Deliver Cambridge curriculum (Primary, Lower Secondary, IGCSE, AS & A Level)
Register students for Cambridge examinations
Administer Cambridge exams under secure and standardized conditions
Receive official Cambridge certificates and results
content.title: What is a Cambridge Centre?

content.text: Centre Number: Each Eduversal Partner School holds a unique Cambridge Centre number (e.g., ID### for Indonesian centres) that identifies the school in all Cambridge systems and correspondence.
Initial Application (for new schools joining the Eduversal network):

items: The school must hold a valid Indonesian operational license
Adequate facilities (classrooms, labs, exam rooms)
Qualified teaching staff
Commitment to Cambridge standards and ethics
Complete the Cambridge Centre application via the Cambridge International Direct website
Provide school details, staffing, and facilities information
Nominate key roles: Centre Head (Principal), Exams Officer, Quality Nominee
Pay application fee (one-time)
Cambridge reviews the application and may request additional information or a site visit
Approval typically takes 4-8 weeks
Upon approval, the school receives the Centre number and login credentials for the Cambridge systems.
Centre status is valid indefinitely, subject to compliance with ongoing requirements

content.text: Existing Centres: All established Eduversal Partner Schools already hold Cambridge Centre status. The school principal ensures continuity by maintaining registration, updating Centre details, and complying with annual requirements.
Requirement: Every year, each Centre must complete and submit an Annual Centre Declaration to Cambridge confirming:

items: Centre contact details are current (address, phone, email)
Key personnel (Centre Head, Exams Officer, Quality Nominee) are up to date
Facilities remain adequate for exam administration
Centre commits to upholding Cambridge standards and exam security
content.title: Annual Centre Declaration

items: Declaration form sent by Cambridge (typically February-March)
Deadline: Before exam entry windows open (varies by series)
GM or Exams Officer completes form online via Cambridge International Direct

content.text: Failure to Complete: Non-submission may result in suspension of Centre status or inability to register students for exams. Evidence:

items: Cambridge Centre number certificate
Annual declaration submission confirmations (past 3 years)
Correspondence with Cambridge regarding Centre status

content.text: Cambridge requires each Centre to designate specific individuals responsible for examination integrity and quality assurance.

items: Overall accountability for Cambridge Centre compliance
Ensure secure exam administration and prevent malpractice
Approve student registrations and exam entries
Sign off on examination results and irregularities (if any)
Liaise with Cambridge regarding Centre matters
Ensure staff understand and follow Cambridge policies
content.title: Centre Head (Principal \- Primary or Secondary School Principal)

items: Final decision-maker at the Centre level for Cambridge-related issues
May delegate day-to-day tasks to the Exams Officer but retains ultimate responsibility

content.text: Appointment: A dedicated staff member (often Administrative Manager, Vice Principal, or senior teacher) appointed by the Centre Head to manage examination operations. Responsibilities: Before Exams:

items: Coordinate exam entries (student registration, subject selection, tier decisions)
Submit entries to Cambridge via Cambridge International Direct by deadlines.
Order secure exam materials (question papers, answer booklets, CDs/USBs)
Schedule exam timetable (resolve clashes for students taking multiple exams)
Arrange exam rooms, seating plans, and invigilation schedules
Train invigilators on the Cambridge exam conduct rules
Communicate exam schedules and instructions to students and parents
content.title: Exams Officer

items: Oversee secure storage of exam materials (locked safe/cabinet; controlled access)
Distribute exam papers to invigilators at designated times (Key Times)
Monitor exam conduct and respond to irregularities (student illness, disruption, suspected malpractice)
Collect completed scripts and secure until dispatch
Package and dispatch scripts to Cambridge (or e-marking upload if applicable)
Submit attendance registers and any incident reports
Communicate results to students when released by Cambridge
Manage post-results services (enquiries about results, access to scripts, appeals)
Archive exam documentation per Cambridge retention requirements (5 years)
Cambridge provides online training modules and resources for Exams Officers
Exams Officer must stay current with Cambridge Handbook updates and attend Eduversal network training sessions
Exams Officer appointment letter
Training completion certificates (Cambridge online modules, Eduversal sessions)
Exam administration checklists and logs (entries, materials, dispatch)
Invigilation schedules and training records

content.text: Purpose: The Quality Nominee supports internal quality assurance and serves as a liaison with Cambridge on standards and curriculum delivery. Appointment: Typically, a senior academic leader (Principal, Vice Principal Academic, or HOD) with a strong understanding of the Cambridge curriculum and assessment. Responsibilities:

items: Monitor teaching quality and curriculum coverage against the Cambridge standards.
Review student work and assessment practices for alignment with Cambridge expectations.
Facilitate internal moderation and standardization
Identify professional development needs for Cambridge curriculum delivery
Liaise with Cambridge on curriculum updates, resources, and support
Participate in Cambridge training and quality assurance initiatives (e.g., webinars, regional meetings)
content.title: Quality Nominee

items: Quality Nominee appointment letter
Meeting notes on curriculum review and quality assurance
Internal moderation records
Participation in Cambridge professional development events

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools deliver the Cambridge curriculum across multiple stages, tailored to student age and progression.

items: Subjects: English, Mathematics, Science
Skills-based approach with clear learning objectives
Inquiry, problem-solving, and application are emphasized
content.title: Cambridge Primary (Ages 5-11)

items: Cambridge Primary Checkpoint: Optional diagnostic tests at the end of Primary (typically Grade 6, age 11)
Tests in English, Mathematics, Science
Provides a feedback report comparing student performance to international benchmarks
Not high-stakes; used for formative purposes and transition planning to Lower Secondary
Cambridge Primary subjects taught in English (EAL support provided as needed)
Indonesian national subjects taught separately (Bahasa Indonesia, PPKn, Religion, Mulok)
Profil Pelajar Pancasila projects and character education are integrated
Subjects: English, Mathematics, Science (core); additional subjects may include ICT, Global Perspectives, Art, Music, PE
Builds on Primary; deepens conceptual understanding and skills
content.title: Cambridge Lower Secondary (Ages 11-14)

items: Cambridge Lower Secondary Checkpoint: Optional tests at end of Lower Secondary (typically Grade 9, age 14)
Tests in English, Mathematics, Science
Provides diagnostic feedback and readiness for IGCSE
Cambridge Lower Secondary as a foundation for IGCSE progression
Students continue Indonesian national subjects to meet MoE requirements
International General Certificate of Secondary Education
Recognized globally as equivalent to national secondary qualifications (e.g., UK GCSE, US Grade 10-11 completion)
Wide range of subjects available (70+ subjects; schools select based on capacity and student interest)
content.title: Cambridge IGCSE (Ages 14-16, Grades 10-11)

content.text: Typical Subject Offering at Eduversal Schools: Core Subjects (Required for most students):

items: English as a Second Language (ESL) or English Literature
Mathematics (Core or Extended tier)
Sciences: Biology, Chemistry, Physics (separate sciences or Combined Science)

content.text: Additional Subjects (Student choice based on interest and university plans):

items: Humanities: Geography, History, Global Perspectives
Languages: Indonesian as a First Language, Mandarin, French (varies by school)
Business & Economics: Business Studies, Economics, Accounting
Creative & Technical: Art & Design, ICT, Computer Science, Design & Technology
Other: Physical Education, Music
Grades: A (highest), A, B, C, D, E, F, G, U (ungraded/fail)
New 9-1 grading scale introduced for some subjects (9 \= A, 1 \= G)
Students typically take 5-10 IGCSEs over two years (Grades 10-11)
Primarily external exams at the end of the two-year course (May/June series in Grade 11)
Some subjects include coursework component (submitted during course; externally moderated by Cambridge)
Tiered exams for some subjects (e.g., Mathematics Core vs Extended)
Advanced Subsidiary (AS) Level: One-year course, typically 3-4 subjects
A Level: Two-year course, typically 3-4 subjects (may include AS as first year)
Depth and rigor comparable to first-year university in some countries
content.title: Cambridge AS & A Level (Ages 16-18, Grades 11-13)

items: Sciences: Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Mathematics, Further Mathematics
Humanities: History, Geography, Economics, Psychology, Sociology
Languages: English Language, English Literature, Indonesian
Business: Business, Accounting
Other: Art & Design, Computer Science, ICT
AS Level: Grades a, b, c, d, e (lowercase to distinguish from A Level)
A Level: Grades A (highest), A, B, C, D, E, U (ungraded)
Primarily external exams (May/June or October/November series)
Some subjects include practical exams (Sciences) or coursework (Art, certain humanities)
Cambridge AS/A Levels widely recognized for university admission globally
Competitive Indonesian universities increasingly accept A Levels
UK, Australia, Canada, US, Europe: strong recognition

content.text: Cambridge offers two main examination series per year. Eduversal Partner Schools typically use both series depending on student needs.
Exam Period: Late April to Early June Results Release: Early-Mid August Typical Students:

items: Grade 10 students completing IGCSE (end of two-year course)
Grade 11 students completing AS Level (end of one-year course)
Grade 12 students completing A Level (end of two-year course, or second year after AS)
content.title: May/June Series (Primary Series for Most Students)

content.text: Key Dates (Based on 2024-2025 cycle; confirm annually with Cambridge):
Exam Period: Late October to Late November Results Release: Mid-January (following year) Typical Students:

items: Students retaking exams to improve grades
Students who missed May/June due to illness or other reasons
A small number of students on accelerated or deferred timelines
content.title: October/November Series (Supplementary Series)

items: Schools may administer Checkpoint tests during October/November or May/June windows (school chooses)
Tests administered by school staff (not invigilated like IGCSE/AS/A)
Scripts sent to Cambridge for marking
Results are typically returned 2-3 months after the test window
content.title: Cambridge Primary & Lower Secondary Checkpoint

content.text: Responsibility: Exams Officer, in coordination with Principal/GM, Academic Coordinators, and students/parents.
IGCSE Example (for students in Grade 9, exams in Grade 10 May/June): Timeline: September-October of Grade 9 Process:

steps: Academic counseling sessions with students and parents
content.title: Step 1: Student Subject Selection (Term before exams)

items: Review student strengths, interests, and university plans
Explain subject requirements and combinations
Discuss tier options (e.g., Mathematics Extended vs Core)
Students select 5-10 subjects from school's offerings
Principal/Academic Coordinator approves based on:
Prerequisite completion
Timetable feasibility
Teacher capacity

steps: Finalize subject list by the end of Term 1 (October-November)

content.text: Timeline: Early April (for May/June series); Early September (for Oct/Nov series) Process:

steps: Exams Officer accesses Cambridge International Direct
content.title: Step 2: Exam Entries Submission (8-10 weeks before exams)

items: Portal for all Centre exam administration
Candidate name (as per passport or national ID)
Date of birth
Candidate number (assigned by Cambridge after first registration)
Subjects and syllabus codes
Tier (if applicable, e.g., Extended vs Core Mathematics)
Components (written papers, practical, coursework)
Request accommodations for students with documented learning needs (e.g., extra time, scribe, rest breaks) \- must be pre-approved by Cambridge (Section 3.9)
Exams Officer reviews entries for accuracy
Centre Head approves submission
Submit by deadline (missing deadline incurs late fees or may prevent registration)
Cambridge issues Entry Confirmation Report
Exams Officer verifies all students and subjects are correct
Communicate to students and parents (exam timetable, fees)
Cambridge charges per subject per student
Fees vary by qualification (IGCSE, AS, A Level) and subject
School typically passes costs to students/parents (included in annual fees or billed separately)
Final Timetable showing date and time for each exam paper (all Centres worldwide follow the same schedule)
Timetable Clashes: Students taking certain subject combinations may have two exams scheduled at the same time
Resolution: One exam taken at the scheduled time; second exam taken later same day in "clash session" (extended day for student and invigilation team)
content.title: Step 3: Timetable Preparation (6-8 weeks before exams)

items: School Exam Timetable with rooms, seating plans, invigilation assignments
Communicate to students (exam dates, times, room assignments)
Communicate to staff (invigilation duties, support roles)

subsections.title: Secure Storage and Exam Materials Management

content.text: Principle: Exam papers are confidential until the Key Time (official start time of the exam). Breaches compromise exam integrity and may result in sanctions.

items: Lockable safe, cabinet, or strong room dedicated to exam materials
Limited access: Exams Officer and Centre Head only (keys held by these individuals)
Room secured with alarm or located in monitored area (e.g., Principal's office, secure admin room)
content.title: Secure Storage Requirements

items: Record every access to secure storage (date, time, person, purpose)
Log maintained throughout exam period and retained for audit
Secure materials shipped from Cambridge (tracked delivery)
Exams Officer verifies package integrity (seals intact, no damage)
Check contents against dispatch list
Sign and date receipt
Store immediately in secure location

content.text: Definition: The official start time of an exam (specified by Cambridge for each paper). Exam papers may not be opened or viewed by anyone before Key Time. Adherence:

items: Exams Officer delivers sealed exam packets to exam room at Key Time (not before)
Invigilator opens packet in front of students at Key Time
Late opening or early opening is malpractice and must be reported
content.title: Key Times

items: Most exams: Key Time is morning (e.g., 08:00 or 09:00 local time) or afternoon (e.g., 13:00 or 14:00)
Some exams (e.g., listening tests): Key Time precisely specified down to the minute

steps: Exams Officer retrieves exam packet from secure storage
Opens in presence of invigilators and students at Key Time
Distributes question papers and answer booklets
Invigilator verifies all students have correct paper and materials
content.title: Exam Paper Distribution

items: Invigilator collects all scripts, question papers (if confidential), and unused materials
Return to Exams Officer immediately
Exams Officer secures scripts until dispatch to Cambridge

content.text: Purpose: Ensure exams are conducted fairly, securely, and in accordance with Cambridge regulations to maintain the credibility of qualifications.

items: Teachers (from any subject, not just the exam subject)
Senior administrators
External invigilators (if school hires additional support)
content.title: Invigilator Qualifications

items: Teacher of the exam subject for that specific exam (conflict of interest; may give unintentional hints)
Close relatives of candidates sitting the exam
Students or untrained staff
All invigilators must complete Cambridge invigilation training (online module or school-based session)
Annual refresher required
Exams Officer provides pre-exam briefing covering specific exam details and contingencies
1 invigilator per 30 students
content.title: Invigilation Ratios

items: 1 invigilator per 20-25 students for better supervision and faster response to student queries
If more than 30 students, assign additional invigilators
Desks arranged with adequate spacing (minimum 1.25 meters between students to prevent copying)
Seating plan displayed (students assigned specific seats by candidate number)
Clock visible to all students
"Examination in Progress \- Silence" signs on doors
content.title: Exam Room Setup

items: Mobile phones, smartwatches, electronic devices (must be switched off and stored away from exam area)
Unauthorized notes, books, dictionaries (unless specified by Cambridge for open-book exams)
Calculators (only approved models allowed per Cambridge list)
Clear water bottle (label removed)
Pens, pencils, erasers, rulers (no cases with printed text)
Calculators (if allowed for subject)
Spec sheets, formula booklets (if provided by Cambridge)
Arrive 15 minutes before Key Time
Present student ID or candidate number
Sit at the assigned seat
Follow all invigilator instructions
No communication with other students once exam begins (speaking, gestures, passing notes)
No cheating or malpractice (see Section 3.10)
content.title: Exam Conduct Rules

items: Arrive 30 minutes before Key Time
Set up room and verify materials
Check student IDs
Distribute papers at Key Time
Monitor students continuously (walk around room quietly)
Respond to student queries about exam instructions (may clarify procedural questions but not content)
Record start and end times
Collect all scripts and materials at end of exam
Report any irregularities immediately
Invigilators must remain alert and vigilant
No reading, phone use, or other distractions by invigilators
If student needs restroom break (extended exams only): Invigilator escorts; no extra time added unless pre-approved accommodation
Exams must start at Key Time (or within 5 minutes if unavoidable delay)
Duration specified by Cambridge (e.g., 1 hour 45 minutes, 2 hours 30 minutes)
5-minute warning before end
No extra time unless pre-approved access arrangement
content.title: Exam Timing

items: If exam starts late due to unavoidable circumstances (e.g., fire alarm), contact Cambridge immediately for guidance
If student has approved extra time (e.g., 25% extra), calculate and implement precisely

content.text: Cambridge provides fair access to exams for students with disabilities, learning differences, or temporary difficulties.
Purpose: Enable students with long-term or permanent needs to demonstrate their abilities without disadvantage. Common Accommodations:

items: Extra time (typically 25% additional time)
Supervised rest breaks
Scribe (someone writes student's dictated answers)
Reader (someone reads questions aloud to student)
Word processor (typing instead of handwriting)
Enlarged print papers
Modified paper format (e.g., Braille, audio)
content.title: Access Arrangements (Pre-Exam Accommodations)

items: Student must have documented disability or learning difference (e.g., dyslexia, ADHD, visual impairment, physical disability)
Assessment by qualified professional (educational psychologist, medical doctor) required
Evidence of need in normal classroom setting (student uses accommodation regularly, not just for exams)

steps: Documentation Collection (at least 6 months before exams):

items: Parents provide diagnostic reports and professional recommendations
School EAL/SEN Coordinator reviews and confirms need

steps: Application to Cambridge (3-4 months before exams):

items: Exams Officer submits Access Arrangement application via Cambridge International Direct
Include evidence (diagnostic reports, school support records)
Specify requested accommodation and rationale

steps: Cambridge Review and Approval (2-3 months before):

items: Cambridge reviews and approves or requests additional information
Approval valid for duration of student's time at Centre (not re-applied annually unless need changes)
Exams Officer ensures approved accommodations are in place for all relevant exams
Separate exam room if needed (e.g., for student using scribe or needing rest breaks)
Invigilator trained on specific accommodation
Access arrangement applications and approvals (stored securely for each student)
Implementation logs (which accommodations used for which exams)
Student support records showing normal use of accommodations

content.text: Purpose: Allow Cambridge to take into account circumstances that may have temporarily affected student's performance. Examples:

items: Illness on exam day (student took exam but was unwell)
Bereavement (death of close family member near exam period)
Serious accident or trauma
Administrative error by Centre (e.g., wrong paper given, timing error)
content.title: Special Considerations (Post-Exam Circumstances)

items: Exams Officer documents circumstances immediately
Student completes exam if possible (or misses exam if unable)

steps: Special Consideration Form (within 7 days of exam):

items: Exams Officer submits form to Cambridge via Cambridge International Direct
Include evidence (medical certificate, incident report, supporting documentation)
Request consideration (not asking for specific grade adjustment; Cambridge decides)
Cambridge reviews case and may:
Allow script to be marked with consideration (small grade adjustment possible)
Declare result void and allow student to retake without penalty (rare)
Take no action if circumstances deemed not to have significantly impacted performance
Special consideration is not guaranteed to change grade
Does not replace need for student to sit exam (unless physically impossible)
Must be applied for promptly (late applications usually rejected)
Special consideration applications and outcomes
Supporting documentation (medical certificates, etc.)
Communication with Cambridge

content.text: Definition: Any action that compromises exam integrity or gives unfair advantage.

items: Cheating: Copying from another student, using unauthorized notes or devices
Plagiarism: Submitting coursework copied from internet or other sources without citation
Collusion: Students working together on individual coursework and submitting same/similar work
Personation: Someone else taking exam for student, or student taking exam for someone else
Disruptive behavior: Deliberately disturbing other students
content.title: Types of Malpractice

items: Opening papers early: Viewing exam before Key Time
Inadequate security: Allowing unauthorized access to exam materials
Failure to report irregularities: Centre aware of issue but does not inform Cambridge
Providing unauthorized assistance: Teacher giving hints or answers during exam
Falsifying coursework marks: Teacher inflating marks for internal assessment
Academic integrity policy explained in student handbook and assemblies
Explicit teaching of citation and referencing for coursework
Consequences of malpractice clearly communicated
content.title: Prevention Strategies

items: Strict adherence to Cambridge storage and Key Time requirements
Multiple invigilators per room
Seating plans that minimize copying opportunities
Prohibition of phones and electronics
Annual refresher on Cambridge conduct rules
Ethical standards emphasized
Reporting obligations clear
Invigilator observes suspicious behavior (looking at another paper, using phone, etc.)
Invigilator reports immediately to Exams Officer
Incident documented (time, student, behavior observed)
Student may be allowed to continue exam (decision by Centre Head/Exams Officer) pending investigation
content.title: Detection and Reporting

items: Cambridge detects similarity in scripts (similar unusual answers or errors)
Teacher or moderator notices plagiarism in coursework
Mandatory: Centre must report all suspected malpractice to Cambridge within 7 days
Failure to report is itself malpractice by Centre
Detailed description of incident
Evidence (invigilator statement, scripts, coursework samples)
Student statement (Centre interviews student; student may admit or deny)
Centre recommendation (sanction or further investigation)

steps: May request additional information from Centre or student
Makes a determination (malpractice occurred or not)
content.title: Cambridge Investigation and Sanctions

items: Warning letter
Disqualification from the subject (no grade awarded)
Disqualification from all subjects in that series (all results invalidated)
Ban from future exams (1-5 years or permanent, depending on severity)
Warning letter
Conditions placed on Centre (enhanced monitoring, restrictions on coursework subjects)
Suspension of Centre status (temporary inability to register students)
Withdrawal of Centre status (permanent; school can no longer offer Cambridge)
Student or Centre may appeal Cambridge decision (process specified by Cambridge)
Incident reports for any irregularities or suspected malpractice
Correspondence with Cambridge regarding investigations
Sanctions imposed and follow-up actions

content.text: Timeline: Scripts must be dispatched to Cambridge within 2 working days of exam completion (or uploaded immediately if e-marking).

items: Invigilator collects all scripts and unused materials
Count scripts (verify number matches student attendance register)
Return to Exams Officer in sealed envelope
Exams Officer organizes scripts by subject and syllabus code
Place in Cambridge-provided packaging or sturdy box
Complete Attendance Register (list of students who sat exam; indicate any absences)
Include any reports (special consideration forms, incident reports)
Send via approved courier (Cambridge provides courier account or instructions)
Obtain tracking number
Retain proof of dispatch
Cambridge confirms receipt
Exams Officer monitors tracking and follows up if delay
Cambridge has transitioned many subjects to e-marking (check annual updates)
e-Marking: Students write on paper as normal; Centre scans scripts and uploads to Cambridge
content.title: e-Marking (Electronic Marking)

items: Use high-quality scanner (specifications provided by Cambridge)
Scan each page of each student's script
Save as PDF
Upload PDFs to Cambridge via secure portal
Deadline: Within 48 hours of exam
Cambridge reviews uploads for legibility
May request re-scan if quality insufficient
Faster results
More efficient dispatch (no physical courier)
Reduced risk of lost scripts
School must have adequate scanning capacity and reliable internet
Training for staff on scanning protocols
Attendance registers (retained by school for 5 years)
Dispatch receipts or upload confirmations
Tracking information
May/June series: Results released early-mid August
Oct/Nov series: Results released mid-January
content.title: Results Release

items: Centre Head and Exams Officer log in to Cambridge International Direct
Download Statement of Results (PDF listing all students' grades)
Individual candidate statements available for printing
School communicates results to students (results day event, email, or student portal)
Confidentiality: Results shared only with student and parents (not publicly posted)
Exams Officer and Principal/GM analyze results:
Overall pass rates and grade distribution
Subject-level performance
Comparison to previous years and international benchmarks (if Cambridge provides)
Identify strengths and areas for improvement (feed into School Improvement Plan)
Cambridge dispatches certificates approximately 3 months after results release
Certificates sent to Centre (not directly to students)
content.title: Certificates

items: School holds a certificate presentation ceremony (typically at graduation or special awards event)
Certificates presented to students in sealed envelope
Student signs register confirming receipt
If certificate lost or damaged, student may request replacement from Cambridge (fee applies)
School retains copy of results for records (certificates belong to students)

content.text: Students or parents may request review of results if they believe an error occurred.
content.title: Post-Results Services

content.text: Purpose: Request Cambridge to re-check marking or return marked script for review. Services Available:

items: Clerical re-check: Verify all questions marked and marks totaled correctly (no re-marking; just checking for administrative errors)
Re-moderation of coursework: For subjects with coursework component; external moderator reviews Centre's marking again
Return of scripts: Student receives photocopy of marked script to see examiner comments (no grade change; for learning purposes)
Priority re-mark: Full re-mark by senior examiner (may result in grade change up or down)
content.title: 1. Enquiry About Results (EAR)

items: Typically 4-6 weeks after results release
Late requests not accepted

steps: Student/parent requests EAR and pays fee (to school or directly to Cambridge, depending on school policy)
Exams Officer submits EAR via Cambridge International Direct
Outcome communicated (grade upheld, raised, or lowered)

items: If re-mark results in grade change (up or down), Cambridge issues revised certificate
Revised grade is final
EAR requests and outcomes logged
Communication with students/parents regarding process and fees
Revised certificates (if grades changed)
Analysis of EAR outcomes (identify any patterns suggesting need for improved teaching/assessment)

content.text: Purpose: Allow students to see their marked exam scripts for learning purposes, even if not requesting a re-mark. Process:

items: Student requests access to scripts (fee applies)
Cambridge provides scanned copies with examiner marks and comments
No grade change; purely for feedback
content.title: 2. Access to Scripts

items: Teachers review with students to identify strengths and areas for improvement
Inform future exam preparation strategies
If student believes Cambridge made procedural error (beyond marking accuracy)
Examples: wrong exam administered, special consideration not properly applied, malpractice sanction unjust
content.title: 3. Appeals

items: Formal appeal to Cambridge with detailed grounds and evidence
Cambridge Appeals Panel reviews
Decision typically final
Support student with documentation if appeal has merit
Neutral stance (not advocate for student but facilitate fair process)

content.text: Purpose: Ensure assessment practices within school align with Cambridge standards, particularly for subjects with coursework or teacher-assessed components.

items: Art & Design: Portfolio of work assessed by teacher
Sciences: Practical skills assessed during course
English Literature: Some syllabi include coursework essay
content.title: Subjects with Coursework/Teacher Assessment

items: Use Cambridge-provided mark schemes and assessment criteria
Assign marks for each student's coursework
Department Head or experienced teacher reviews sample of marked work
Check for:
Consistency (similar quality work receiving similar marks)
Accuracy (marks align with criteria)
Rank order (relative performance of students correct)
Discuss discrepancies and adjust marks if needed
Exams Officer uploads teacher-assessed marks via Cambridge International Direct
Deadline typically 1-2 months before written exams
Cambridge selects sample of students' work for Centre to submit (often highest, middle, lowest achieving students)
Cambridge moderator reviews and compares Centre's marks to their independent assessment
If discrepancy found, Cambridge may adjust all marks for that subject up or down to align with standard
Teacher marks combined with external exam marks (weighting specified by Cambridge)
Final grade issued
Before assessing coursework or internal assessments
At start of each marking period
content.title: Standardization Meetings

items: Ensure all teachers of same subject interpret mark schemes consistently

steps: Teachers review mark schemes and assessment criteria together

items: Standardization meeting agendas and notes
Sample marked work (annotated with marks and rationale)
Internal moderation reports
External moderation feedback from Cambridge

content.text: Purpose: Support teachers in delivering high-quality Cambridge curriculum and assessment.

items: Cambridge Professional Development Qualification (PDQ): Teaching & Learning; Leadership
Cambridge International Certificate in Teaching and Learning: Subject-specific (e.g., Teaching Mathematics)
Online courses: Self-paced modules on Cambridge pedagogy, assessment, specific subjects
content.title: Cambridge Professional Development Qualifications

items: Eduversal subsidizes or fully funds Cambridge PD for key staff
Target: All HODs and core subject teachers complete at least one Cambridge PD qualification
Tracked via HR professional development records
Access via login credentials (each Centre has access)
Resources available:
Syllabi and specimen papers: Current and past exam papers with mark schemes
Schemes of Work: Suggested teaching sequences and lesson plans
Teacher Guides: Detailed guidance on pedagogy and assessment
Learner Guides: Resources for students
Past Papers: Historical exams for practice
content.title: Cambridge Online Resources

items: Teachers download resources for planning and instruction
Students access past papers for exam preparation
School may purchase additional resources (textbooks, online platforms like Cambridge GO)
Records of Cambridge PD course completion
Lesson plans incorporating Cambridge resources
Student exam preparation materials (past papers, practice tests)
Cambridge offers free webinars throughout year on curriculum updates, assessment, pedagogy
Recorded sessions available for later viewing
content.title: Cambridge Webinars and Regional Training

items: Occasional face-to-face workshops in Indonesia or region (Jakarta, Singapore)
Eduversal may coordinate attendance for multiple schools
Monitors Cambridge training opportunities
Shares information with staff
Encourages participation

content.text: Purpose: Recognition program for schools demonstrating excellence in Cambridge education. Award Levels:

items: Registered: School applies and commits to the development plan
Accredited: School meets quality benchmarks across teaching, assessment, student support
Outstanding: Highest level; exemplary practice
External validation of quality
Marketing advantage (logo, certificate)
Access to exclusive Cambridge resources and events
Professional development opportunities
School applies via Cambridge International
Conduct self-assessment against award criteria
Submit evidence portfolio
Cambridge reviews and awards appropriate level
Valid for 3 years; renewable
All established partner schools achieve Accredited level
Flagship schools pursue Outstanding level
Cambridge International School Award application and status
Self-assessment documentation
Award certificate and renewal tracking
Comprehensive guide to Centre administration, exam regulations, quality assurance
Exams Officer and Centre Head must review annually
Available on Cambridge School Support Hub
content.title: Key Cambridge Policies (Schools Must Follow):

items: Cambridge requires all Centres to have child protection policy
Students taking exams must be in safe environment
Reporting obligations if safeguarding concerns arise
Student data shared with Cambridge must be secure
Cambridge GDPR-compliant (for UK/EU data laws); Centres must follow local laws (Indonesia's PDP Law)
Exam questions and materials are copyrighted by Cambridge
Schools may not reproduce or share exam papers publicly (except past papers released by Cambridge)
Violation \= breach of Centre agreement

content.text: 5. Marketing and Use of Cambridge Name:

items: Schools may state they are Cambridge Centre
May use Cambridge logo with permission
May not misrepresent relationship (e.g., cannot claim "Cambridge University-affiliated")
All marketing materials mentioning Cambridge must be accurate
☐ Review updated Cambridge Handbook (released annually)
☐ Update Centre details in Cambridge International Direct (staff changes, contact info)
☐ Submit Annual Centre Declaration (if due)
☐ Confirm exam series usage for academic year (May/June and/or Oct/Nov)
☐ EduOS Cambridge prep sessions commence (for students targeting Olympiad alongside Cambridge)
content.title: Annual Compliance Checklist (Cambridge-Specific)

items: ☐ May/June exam entries window opens (October)
☐ Coursework deadlines approaching for some subjects (monitor and submit)
☐ Checkpoint exams administered (if school opts for Oct/Nov window)
☐ Order secure materials for May/June series
☐ May/June exam entries deadline (early April) \- CRITICAL
☐ Finalize timetable and invigilation schedules
☐ Conduct mock exams (February-March) for May/June candidates
☐ Train/retrain invigilators
☐ Oct/Nov series results released (mid-January) \- analyze and communicate
☐ May/June exams administered (late April \- early June)
☐ Secure materials received and stored
☐ Scripts dispatched or uploaded within 2 days
☐ Oct/Nov exam entries window opens (April-May)
☐ Post-exam review and improvement planning
☐ Access arrangements applications (as students identified)
☐ Special considerations submissions (within 7 days of incident)
☐ Malpractice reporting (immediate)
☐ Quality Nominee liaison with Cambridge (webinars, updates)

content.text: Purpose: Cambridge may conduct inspections or monitoring visits to ensure Centres comply with standards. Types of Visits: 1. Routine Quality Assurance Visit:

items: Random selection or based on Centre performance data
Advance notice (typically 4-6 weeks)
Focus: Facilities, exam security, staff training, student support
Triggered by concerns (e.g., malpractice allegation, pattern of irregularities, complaint)
May be unannounced or short notice
More intensive scrutiny
Before granting Centre status to new school
Verify application information
Exam rooms (capacity, seating, lighting, noise control)
Secure storage (adequate for volume of materials)
Accessibility (accommodations for students with disabilities)
content.title: What Cambridge Inspectors Review:

items: Qualifications and training records (Exams Officer, invigilators, Quality Nominee)
Understanding of Cambridge policies
Exam administration logs (entries, dispatch, results)
Access arrangement approvals and implementation
Malpractice incident reports and follow-up
Internal moderation records
Interview students about exam conditions, support, fairness
Review student feedback or complaints
Confirm dates and logistics with Cambridge
Inform staff and prepare team
Compile all required documents (see Section 3.19 Key Evidence)
Identify and address any gaps
Conduct internal mock inspection
Ensure secure storage is compliant (locks working, access log current)
Verify exam rooms are in good condition
Prepare tour route
Inform staff of visit purpose and process
Review expectations and roles
Emphasize transparency and cooperation
Designate liaison (typically Exams Officer or GM) to accompany inspectors
Provide requested documents promptly
Answer questions honestly
Take notes on feedback and recommendations
Review inspection report (Cambridge sends within 4 weeks)
Develop action plan for any deficiencies or recommendations
Implement improvements and document progress
Submit follow-up report to Cambridge if required
Commendation letter
Confirmation of Centre status
Recognition as high-performing Centre
Recommendations for improvement (school given timeline to address)
Conditions placed on Centre (e.g., increased monitoring, restricted subjects)
Warning letter
Suspension of Centre status (temporary)
Withdrawal of Centre status (permanent) \- rare; only for major breaches

content.text: Challenge: Eduversal Partner Schools deliver Cambridge curriculum while meeting Indonesian national requirements. Balancing both is critical.

items: Cambridge Subjects: \~25-30 periods/week (English, Math, Sciences, Humanities, etc.) \- taught in English
Indonesian National Subjects: \~10-12 periods/week (Bahasa Indonesia, PPKn, Religion, Mulok) \- taught in Bahasa Indonesia
Profil Pelajar Pancasila Projects & Character Education: \~3-5 periods/week (integrated or dedicated time)
Co-curricular/PE/Arts: \~5 periods/week
content.title: Curriculum Timetable Balance

items: Lists Cambridge subjects (IGCSE, AS/A Level)
Grades/predicted grades based on Cambridge standards
Teacher comments on progress toward Cambridge exams
Language: English
content.title: Reporting to Parents: Dual Transcripts

items: Lists Indonesian mandatory subjects (Bahasa Indonesia, PPKn, Religion, Mulok)
Grades based on Indonesian curriculum standards (0-100 scale)
Profil Pelajar Pancasila competency tracking
Language: Bahasa Indonesia
Some schools combine both in one document with separate sections
Clearly labeled to avoid confusion
Bilingual summaries
Term reports (mid-semester and end-semester): Both Cambridge and national subjects
Cambridge results (August/January): Official Cambridge certificates sent separately
Students use national subject grades and IGCSE/AS/A Level certificates
Some top universities (UI, ITB, UGM) recognize Cambridge directly
School provides equivalency letters if needed
content.title: University Admission Support

items: Students use Cambridge IGCSE/AS/A Level results
School provides transcripts, predicted grades, and recommendation letters
University counseling team supports applications (Section 23)

content.text: Compliance Portfolio for Cambridge Centre Status: Centre Registration and Administration:

items: Cambridge Centre number certificate
Annual Centre Declaration submissions (past 3 years)
Centre Head, Exams Officer, Quality Nominee appointment letters
Contact information (current)
Exam entry submissions (past 3 years) via Cambridge International Direct
Entry confirmation reports
Exam timetables and seating plans
Invigilation schedules and training records
Secure storage access logs
Materials receipt and dispatch records
Attendance registers
Access arrangement applications and approvals (per student)
Special consideration submissions and outcomes
Evidence files (medical reports, diagnostic assessments)
Incident reports (if any)
Correspondence with Cambridge regarding investigations
Sanctions imposed and follow-up actions (if any)
Statements of Results (past 5 years)
Results analysis reports
Certificate distribution register
EAR (Enquiry About Results) requests and outcomes
Internal moderation records for coursework subjects
Standardization meeting notes
External moderation feedback from Cambridge
Teaching observations and coaching notes (Cambridge-specific focus)
Cambridge PD course completion certificates
Webinar attendance records
Quality Nominee liaison logs
Inspection reports (if visited)
Action plans and implementation evidence
Cambridge Handbook (current version) with annotations
School Exams Policy (aligned with Cambridge requirements)
Academic Integrity Policy (anti-malpractice)
Cambridge Centre Requirements: Full compliance with Cambridge Assessment International Education Handbook, exam regulations, and quality standards
CIS Standards: Teaching & Assessing for Learning (assessment integrity, external validation); Curriculum (international standards)
WASC Category B: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment (rigor, external benchmarking, valid assessment)
Indonesian Regulations: Integration with national curriculum; recognition of Cambridge qualifications by Indonesian universities and authorities
